title: Tapping for calm mornings
subtitle: A tapping practice for when the day starts in fight-or-flight before you've made a single decision.
excerpt: A ten-minute tapping practice from Day 21 of the SLEEP program. Two rounds: one for starting the day without the rush, one for ending the evening with the same quality of calm.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 21 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use the daytime tap first thing in the morning. Use the night tap in bed as the final practice before sleep.

content.text: A ten-minute tapping practice from Day 21 of the SLEEP program. This day has two sessions: a daytime tap for starting the morning from a grounded place rather than a reactive one, and a night tap for closing the day with that same quality.
New to tapping? Read
how EFT tapping works
first.
The practice
Daytime tap: start the day without the rush
Karate chop
Even though my mornings often begin in a blur of movement, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I wake up already thinking about what needs to be done, I honour the part of me that wants to feel prepared.
Even though I've built a habit of racing the clock, I'm open to creating mornings that start from peace.
Tapping round (repeat x3)
**Eyebrow:** I've been waking up in fight-or-flight for as long as I can remember.
**Side of eye:** My brain starts straight away.
**Under eye:** It's like I'm already behind before the day even starts.
**Under nose:** That rush burns through my calm.
**Chin:** I don't want to live with that default anymore.
**Collarbone:** I want to wake up and actually feel calm.
**Under arm:** I can still be productive without the panic.
**Top of head:** I can start slow and still get everything done.
Reframe to positive (repeat x3)
**Eyebrow:** I begin the day on my own terms.
**Side of eye:** I take a moment to breathe before I do.
**Under eye:** Calm mornings make calm choices.
**Under nose:** I give myself permission to start softly.
**Chin:** The world can wait while I collect myself.
**Collarbone:** My best energy comes from grounded beginnings.
**Under arm:** I choose peace as my first act of the day.
**Top of head:** I'm learning that slow is powerful.
Night tap: match the ending to the beginning
Karate chop
Even though I often fall into bed with my mind still buzzing, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though the day has been full and my body is tired, I honour that I've done my best.
Even though I forget to slow down before bed, I'm open to ending today with the same calm I wanted this morning.
Tapping round (repeat x3)
**Eyebrow:** My brain is still halfway through the to-do list.
**Side of eye:** I feel that low-grade hum of urgency.
**Under eye:** The truth is, the day's work is done whether I finish the list or not.
**Under nose:** I can close the door on it for now.
**Chin:** My body deserves a full stop, not a pause.
**Collarbone:** I can rest without guilt or worry.
**Under arm:** I've done enough, and that's enough.
**Top of head:** I let my body unwind all the way down.
Reframe to positive (repeat x3)
**Eyebrow:** I'm proud of what I managed today.
**Side of eye:** I release everything unfinished until morning.
**Under eye:** My rest is part of my success.
**Under nose:** I let peace close the day for me.
**Chin:** I earned the right to relax completely.
**Collarbone:** I let my breath soften every edge.
**Under arm:** The night is mine to enjoy.
**Top of head:** I fall asleep grateful and content.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Adapted from Day 21 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025). EFT tapping developed by Gary Craig in the 1990s.
